message:
feat(api,connector,observability): expand fleet quarantine, evidence ledger, and 4 connector modules

- api/fleet_quarantine.rs: extend quarantine API
- connector/eviction_saga.rs: refine eviction saga
- connector/region_ownership.rs: extend ownership logic
- connector/transport_fault_gate.rs: refine fault gate
- main.rs: wiring updates
- observability/evidence_ledger.rs: major expansion (+386/-106 lines)
- runtime/anti_entropy.rs: minor fix
- .beads/issues.jsonl: sync tracker state

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1now.5.2","title":"Add anti-drift safeguards, examples, and consistency checks for semantic-boundary policy","description":"Why this exists:
A classification matrix without anti-drift proof obligations will decay as soon as new local helpers, wrappers, or canonical imports get added under delivery pressure.

What this task must do:
Add the anti-drift layer for the semantic-boundary policy.

Depending on the surfaces involved, that anti-drift layer may include:
- deterministic unit tests or snapshot tests that protect key local-vs-canonical classification tables,
- examples / contributor-facing decision cases that show when a new concept stays local versus when it must reference canonical upstream ownership,
- cheap checkers that fail when a forbidden duplicate/fork pattern or undocumented boundary crossing reappears,
- stable failure logging or reason codes so policy violations are easy to triage.

Why this matters:
This is what makes the semantic-boundary policy reusable and enforceable rather than a one-time document.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The semantic-boundary policy has deterministic anti-drift safeguards appropriate to the actual surfaces involved (tests, snapshots, examples, checkers, or a justified combination).
2. The safeguards make it obvious when a contributor is creating an undocumented duplicate fork or boundary crossing.
3. Failure output is detailed enough for contributors to understand what changed and which policy rule they violated.
4. The safeguards export stable rule ids/messages and, where applicable, machine-readable fields that the repo-level proof harness can reuse instead of inventing a second policy vocabulary.
5. This bead delivers the implementation-layer safeguards; the repo-level scripted/E2E proof lane is tracked separately.
6. If cargo-heavy validation is needed, it is executed through `rch`.","notes":"Do not overfit this to one current file layout. The anti-drift layer should help future contributors make correct ownership decisions even if files move or additional local semantic surfaces appear.

Keep the implementation-layer checks composable so the later repo-level proof harness can reuse the same rule ids, messages, and artifacts instead of inventing parallel policy logic.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CalmSnow","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:47.853428848Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T17:04:53.663068581Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T17:04:53.663043164Z","close_reason":"Completed semantic-boundary anti-drift safeguards, rule catalog, and checker/test coverage","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","boundaries","self-contained","test-obligations","testing","verification-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.5.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:47.853428848Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.5.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.5.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:48.264057922Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.5.3","title":"Add repo-level semantic-boundary proof harness with E2E cases and detailed logs","description":"Why this exists:
The semantic-boundary policy will not stay trustworthy if it only exists as a matrix plus low-level checks. The repo needs a scripted proof lane that demonstrates both forbidden and allowed cases with detailed, reusable diagnostics.

What this task must do:
Add a repository-level validation harness for the semantic-boundary policy, with deterministic positive and negative cases.

The harness should exercise at minimum:
- a forbidden duplicate/fork case that must fail,
- an undocumented boundary crossing case that must fail,
- at least one explicitly allowed local-model/spec case that must pass,
- at least one canonical-alignment case that must pass or route to the documented upstream rule.

Required outputs:
- at least one deterministic script under `tests/e2e/` or a tightly justified equivalent repo-level harness,
- machine-readable summary output,
- human-readable detailed logs/artifacts,
- stable reason codes / rule ids that map directly back to the semantic-boundary policy.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The semantic-boundary cluster has deterministic repository-level validation coverage for both forbidden and allowed cases.
2. The harness emits both machine-readable summaries and human-readable detailed logs/artifacts.
3. Failures identify the violated boundary rule with stable reason codes / rule ids and enough context for contributors to fix the issue without policy archaeology.
4. Machine-readable outputs carry stable case identifiers, schema/version markers, and artifact locations that map back to the semantic-boundary matrix and anti-drift rule ids.
5. The harness is reusable by the closeout gate and future regressions, not a one-off proof script.
6. Any cargo-heavy or broad validation required by this lane is executed through `rch`.","notes":"Treat this like a policy E2E lane, not a documentation demo.  {"id":"bd-1now.6","title":"Deferred trigger: adopt native Asupersync request-region boundaries when a real async server lands","description":"Why this exists:
The audit deliberately rejected an immediate native-Asupersync migration of the control-plane service skeleton because `crates/franken-node/src/api/service.rs` is still a skeleton/catalog layer rather than a live async HTTP or gRPC server boundary.

What this deferred task must preserve for future self:
If `franken_node` later grows a real async network boundary, that is the moment to revisit native Asupersync request-region/capability-narrowing patterns.

Future trigger conditions:
- real socket accept loop or gRPC call boundary is introduced in this crate,
- request cancellation and per-request lifecycle become real runtime concerns,
- middleware begins owning spawn/time/IO authority rather than just metadata,
- the crate needs deterministic request shutdown/drain semantics.

When the trigger fires, the future implementation should evaluate:
- request-region ownership,
- narrowed request `Cx` capability exposure,
- explicit lifecycle/Outcome mapping at transport boundaries,
- whether the crate should own an `AppSpec`-like supervised service topology.

Why this bead exists now:
It prevents future contributors from forgetting that the right answer for HTTP/gRPC boundaries may eventually be native Asupersync — while also making clear that today is not that day.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The deferred bead captures the exact trigger conditions that would justify revisiting native Asupersync request/call boundary patterns in `franken_node`.
2. The bead makes it explicit that the current service skeleton is not yet sufficient justification for a migration.
3. Future contributors can pick this bead up without needing to rediscover why the work was deferred, what proof obligations wake it up, and what evidence shape the eventual cluster must produce.","notes":"This bead should remain deferred until the crate owns a real async server boundary. - `rch`-offloaded heavy cargo validation.","status":"deferred","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:44.260873625Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T04:29:50.832026969Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","deferred-trigger","grpc","http","runtime","self-contained"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.6","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:44.260873625Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.6","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.5.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:39:57.353548031Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1now.9","title":"bd-1now support: make telemetry bridge drain stop-aware for idle open clients","description":"Why this exists: crates/franken-node/src/ops/telemetry_bridge.rs::handle_connection() reads accepted UnixStream clients with blocking BufRead::lines(). stop() only flips a flag, so an idle-but-open client can keep a connection worker blocked forever waiting for another line. During drain, TelemetryRuntimeHandle::join() waits on those workers and can report a false drain-timeout/failure even though the bridge has no pending work to flush. What this task must do: make the telemetry bridge connection loop stop-aware during drain without dropping normal line-oriented ingestion, keep scope limited to ops/telemetry_bridge.rs, and add a regression proving stop_and_join() completes cleanly when a client stays connected but idle. Acceptance criteria: 1. A connection worker exits promptly after stop() even if the client keeps the socket open and sends no further newline-delimited events. 2. Normal line-delimited event ingestion still succeeds. 3. A focused regression test covers the idle-open-client drain case. 4. Validation uses only light local checks plus rch for any cargo-heavy run if needed.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"CalmMoose","created_at":"2026-03-15T01:36:58.200276434Z","created_by":"CalmMoose","updated_at":"2026-03-15T03:28:59.297609216Z","closed_at":"2026-03-15T03:28:59.297584740Z","close_reason":"Fixed stop-aware idle-client drain path in telemetry bridge; added regression; validated via rch","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["drain","reliability","runtime","shutdown","telemetry"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.9","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-15T01:36:58.200276434Z","created_by":"CalmMoose","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1o4v","title":"[10.18] Implement proof-verification gate API for control-plane trust decisions.","description":"## Why This Exists

Section 10.18 implements the Verifiable Execution Fabric (VEF) — Enhancement Map 9L — delivering proof-carrying runtime compliance. This is a Track C (Trust-Native Ecosystem Layer) critical gate that makes franken_node's trust decisions depend on cryptographic verification rather than trust-by-assertion.

This bead implements the proof-verification gate API — the component that control-plane trust decisions invoke to validate proofs before authorizing high-risk actions or trust transitions. The proof-generation service (bd-1u8m) produces proofs; this gate validates them. It is the trust boundary between "a proof exists" and "the proof is valid and binds to the correct policy and receipt window."

This gate is security-critical: it must be fail-closed (invalid or missing proofs default to denial), must validate the full binding chain (proof -> receipt-window commitment -> policy hash), and must produce stable verdict classes that downstream components (bd-8qlj for control transitions, bd-3pds for verifier SDK) can reason about deterministically.

## What This Must Do

1. Implement a verification gate API that accepts a proof envelope (from bd-1u8m) and validates it against the receipt-window commitment and policy hash binding.
2. Validate the full binding chain: proof envelope -> proof validity -> receipt-window commitment match -> policy hash match.
3. Return stable, classified verdict types: `VALID`, `INVALID_PROOF`, `MISSING_PROOF`, `STALE_POLICY`, `COMMITMENT_MISMATCH`, `VERIFICATION_ERROR`.
4. Enforce fail-closed semantics: any verification failure or missing input defaults to denial with a specific verdict class.
5. Support multiple proof backends transparently — the gate verifies proofs regardless of which backend generated them, using backend-specific verification parameters from the proof envelope.
6. Produce auditable verification records: every gate invocation (pass or fail) emits a signed, replayable verification record.
7. Expose a synchronous API suitable for hot-path integration in control-plane trust decisions.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Verification gate validates proof, receipt-window commitment, and policy hash binding; invalid/missing proofs return stable fail-closed verdict classes.
- All verdict classes are stable, enumerated, and documented with remediation guidance.
- Fail-closed behavior: missing proof -> `MISSING_PROOF` denial; malformed proof -> `INVALID_PROOF` denial; expired policy binding -> `STALE_POLICY` denial.
- Multi-backend support: proofs from different backends validate correctly through the same gate API.
- Verification records are signed and contain enough context for independent replay.
- Gate latency is bounded and suitable for synchronous control-plane integration.
- No silent pass-through: the gate never returns an affirmative verdict without completing full validation.

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests for each verdict class: valid proof, invalid proof, missing proof, stale policy, commitment mismatch, verification error.
- Fail-closed tests: null inputs, partial inputs, corrupted proof bytes — all must return denial verdicts.
- Multi-backend tests: validate proofs from the reference backend and any additional backends through the same gate.
- Binding chain tests: modify the receipt-window commitment after proof generation — must detect mismatch; modify policy hash — must detect stale policy.
- Replay tests: verification records contain sufficient context to re-execute the verification and reach the same verdict.
- Latency benchmark: measure gate verification time under load, confirm it stays within control-plane hot-path budget.
- Structured logging: `VEF-VERIFY-001` (verification requested), `VEF-VERIFY-002` (verdict issued), `VEF-VERIFY-ERR-*` (per verdict class).
- Trace correlation IDs linking verification requests to proof job IDs, receipt window ranges, and originating action contexts.

## Expected Artifacts

- `src/trust/vef_verification_gate.rs` — proof-verification gate API implementation.
- `tests/security/vef_verification_gate.rs` — security-focused tests for all verdict classes and fail-closed behavior.
- `artifacts/10.18/vef_verification_gate_report.json` — verification gate test report with verdict class coverage.
- `artifacts/section_10_18/bd-1o4v/verification_evidence.json` — machine-readable CI/release gate evidence.
- `artifacts/section_10_18/bd-1o4v/verification_summary.md` — human-readable summary linking intent to measured outcomes.

## Dependencies

- bd-1u8m (blocks) — Proof-generation service interface: produces the proof envelopes that this gate validates.

Dependents: bd-8qlj (VEF integration into control transitions), bd-3pds (verifier SDK integration), bd-2hjg (section gate), bd-32p (plan-level tracker).","acceptance_criteria":"- Verification gate validates proof, receipt-window commitment, and policy hash binding; invalid/missing proofs return stable fail-closed verdict classes.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:37:04.625740872Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T07:05:58.155167786Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T07:05:58.155130326Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-18","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1o4v","depends_on_id":"bd-1u8m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T17:05:51.460063573Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1rff","title":"[12] Risk control: longitudinal privacy/re-identification","description":"Master Plan Source: PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKEN_NODE.md
Section: 12

Task Objective:
Implement privacy-preserving trajectory sketching and cohort-size publication thresholds with federated temporal checks.

Execution Requirements:
- Make this requirement machine-verifiable and release-gated where applicable.
- Keep behavior self-documenting so future contributors do not need to re-open the master plan.

Testing & Logging Requirements:
- Unit tests for rule/contract logic and error conditions.
- Integration/E2E tests for workflow-level enforcement.
- Structured logs with stable codes and trace IDs.
- Reproducible artifacts that prove contract satisfaction.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Success and failure invariants for [12] Risk control: longitudinal privacy/re-identification are explicitly quantified and machine-verifiable.
- Determinism requirements for [12] Risk control: longitudinal privacy/re-identification are validated across repeated runs and adversarial perturbations.


Expected Artifacts:
- Machine-readable verification artifact with explicit canonical path under artifacts/section_12/bd-1rff/verification_evidence.json, suitable for CI/release gating.
- Human-readable verification summary with explicit canonical path under artifacts/section_12/bd-1rff/verification_summary.md, linking implementation intent to measured validation outcomes.

Task-Specific Clarification:
- The implementation must deliver the exact capability named in the title, with no scope dilution and no silent feature omission.
- Validation artifacts must include capability-specific thresholds, pass/fail criteria, and reproducible evidence bundles tied to this bead ID.

 {"id":"bd-1ru2","title":"[10.14] Implement cancel-safe eviction saga (upload -> verify -> retire) with deterministic compensations.","description":"## Why This Exists
The L2->L3 artifact lifecycle involves a multi-step saga: upload the artifact to L3, verify the upload succeeded (retrievability proof from bd-1fck), then retire the L2 copy. If any step fails or the process is cancelled (operator interrupt, node crash, network partition), the system must not leave artifacts in a partially-retired state — that would mean the L2 copy is gone but L3 is not confirmed, resulting in data loss. The 9J enhancement map requires a cancel-safe eviction saga with deterministic compensations: every incomplete saga must roll back to a known-good state, and the compensation logic must be provably leak-free (zero orphan states after any failure sequence).

## What This Must Do
1. Implement `EvictionSaga` state machine in `crates/franken-node/src/storage/eviction_saga.rs` (or `src/connector/eviction_saga.rs`) with phases: `Uploading`, `Verifying`, `Retiring`, `Complete`, and `Compensating`.
2. Phase transitions must be persisted to durable storage so that crash recovery can resume or compensate from the last committed phase.
3. Compensation logic: if cancellation or crash occurs during `Uploading`, abort the upload (no L2 change); during `Verifying`, abort and clean up any partial L3 state; during `Retiring`, the L3 copy is confirmed so retirement can proceed on recovery. Compensation must be deterministic — same crash state always produces same recovery action.
4. Require `RemoteCap` capability token (from bd-1nfu) for the upload phase since it involves network-bound operations.
5. Implement leak detection: after saga completion or compensation, verify that no orphan artifacts exist (L2 retired but L3 absent, or L3 present but L2 not retired). Leak detection must run as a post-saga invariant check.
6. Write specification at `docs/specs/eviction_saga.md` covering saga phases, compensation matrix, crash recovery protocol, and leak detection invariants.
7. Produce saga trace artifact at `artifacts/10.14/eviction_saga_trace.jsonl` (JSON Lines format) recording each phase transition with timestamps for audit.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Saga guarantees no partial retire on cancellation/crash; L2 is never retired unless L3 is confirmed retrievable.
- Compensation path is deterministic: identical crash state always triggers identical recovery action.
- Leak tests confirm zero orphan states after any failure sequence (upload failure, verify failure, crash during retire).
- Saga requires RemoteCap for network-bound upload phase.
- Phase transitions are persisted; crash recovery resumes from last committed phase.
- Saga trace (JSONL) records all phase transitions with timestamps, artifact IDs, and outcome codes.

## Testing & Logging Requirements
- **Unit tests**: State machine transition validity (only legal transitions allowed); compensation logic for each crash point; leak detection invariant check with synthetic orphan states.
- **Integration tests**: `tests/integration/eviction_saga_cancel_safety.rs` — full saga success path; saga cancelled during upload (verify L2 intact, L3 cleaned up); saga crashed during verify (verify L2 intact); saga crashed during retire (verify recovery completes retirement); concurrent saga on same artifact (verify mutual exclusion).
- **Leak tests**: Post-saga invariant check that scans all tiers for orphan artifacts; test with injected orphan state to verify detection.
- **Event codes**: `ES_SAGA_START` (saga initiated), `ES_PHASE_UPLOAD` / `ES_PHASE_VERIFY` / `ES_PHASE_RETIRE` (phase transitions), `ES_SAGA_COMPLETE` (success), `ES_COMPENSATION_START` / `ES_COMPENSATION_COMPLETE` (rollback), `ES_LEAK_CHECK_PASSED` / `ES_LEAK_CHECK_FAILED` (post-saga invariant), `ES_CRASH_RECOVERY` (resuming from persisted state).
- **Replay fixture**: Deterministic saga execution with injected failures at each phase boundary.

## Expected Artifacts
- `docs/specs/eviction_saga.md` — specification document
- `tests/integration/eviction_saga_cancel_safety.rs` — integration test suite
- `artifacts/10.14/eviction_saga_trace.jsonl` — saga trace log
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-1ru2/verification_evidence.json` — CI/release gating evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-1ru2/verification_summary.md` — human-readable summary

## Dependencies
- **Depends on**: bd-1fck (retrievability-before-eviction proofs — gate called during verify phase), bd-1nfu (RemoteCap — required for upload phase)
- **Depended on by**: bd-876n (cancellation injection testing), bd-3epz (section gate), bd-5rh (section roll-up)","acceptance_criteria":"Saga guarantees no partial retire on cancellation/crash; compensation path is deterministic; leak tests confirm zero orphan states.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:57.569198947Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T01:19:56.798211377Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T01:19:56.798172093Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-14","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1ru2","depends_on_id":"bd-1fck","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:43:15.442435676Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1ru2","depends_on_id":"bd-1nfu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T16:24:04.735728519Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-3cs3","title":"[10.14] Implement epoch-scoped key derivation for trust artifact authentication.","description":"## Why This Exists

Epoch-scoped key derivation ensures that cryptographic keys used for trust artifact authentication are bound to a specific epoch and domain, making cross-epoch key reuse impossible by construction. This is critical for the 9J security model: if a key derived in epoch N could authenticate artifacts in epoch N+1, an attacker who compromises a key could forge future-epoch artifacts, defeating the entire epoch barrier system. This bead directly enforces runtime invariant #7 (epoch barriers are cryptographically hard) and #8 (evidence-by-default: every trust artifact carries epoch-bound authentication). It depends on the monotonic epoch (bd-3hdv) and feeds into the epoch transition barrier (bd-2wsm) which needs epoch-scoped keys to authenticate barrier participation.

## What This Must Do

1. Implement `EpochKeyDerivation` module in `src/security/epoch_scoped_keys.rs` that derives authentication keys from a root secret, epoch value, and domain string using a standard KDF (e.g., HKDF-SHA256).
2. The derivation function signature: `derive_epoch_key(root_secret: &RootSecret, epoch: ControlEpoch, domain: &str) -> DerivedKey` where `DerivedKey` is a fixed-size key type.
3. Enforce by construction that `derive_epoch_key(secret, epoch_a, domain) != derive_epoch_key(secret, epoch_b, domain)` for any `epoch_a != epoch_b` (this is a property of HKDF with distinct info strings, but must be verified by test vectors).
4. Enforce domain separation: `derive_epoch_key(secret, epoch, "marker") != derive_epoch_key(secret, epoch, "manifest")` for any epoch.
5. Publish verification vectors: a JSON file containing `(root_secret_hex, epoch, domain, expected_key_hex)` tuples that any implementation can verify against.
6. Implement `verify_epoch_signature(artifact: &[u8], signature: &Signature, epoch: ControlEpoch, domain: &str, root_secret: &RootSecret) -> Result<(), AuthError>` that derives the key and verifies the signature.
7. Ensure key material is zeroized on drop (use `zeroize` crate or equivalent).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Authentication key derivation binds to epoch and domain; cross-epoch key reuse is impossible by construction; verification vectors are published.
- For any two distinct `(epoch, domain)` pairs, derived keys differ (verified against published vectors with >= 10 test cases).
- `verify_epoch_signature` rejects a signature produced with a different epoch's key.
- `verify_epoch_signature` rejects a signature produced with a different domain's key.
- Key material is zeroized after use (verified by memory inspection test or `zeroize` derive).
- Published verification vectors in `artifacts/10.14/epoch_key_vectors.json` contain at least 10 vector entries with known-answer tests.

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests: key derivation produces expected output for known inputs (KAT); cross-epoch keys differ; cross-domain keys differ; signature verification succeeds for matching epoch/domain; signature verification fails for mismatched epoch; signature verification fails for mismatched domain; zeroization on drop.
- Integration tests: full sign-verify round trip across epoch transition; key derivation performance (must derive >= 10,000 keys/sec).
- Conformance tests: `tests/conformance/epoch_key_derivation.rs` -- normative test running all published verification vectors.
- Structured logs: `EPOCH_KEY_DERIVED` (epoch, domain, key_fingerprint, trace_id), `EPOCH_SIG_VERIFIED` (artifact_id, epoch, domain, trace_id), `EPOCH_SIG_REJECTED` (artifact_id, epoch, domain, rejection_reason, trace_id). Key material NEVER appears in logs.

## Expected Artifacts

- `src/security/epoch_scoped_keys.rs` -- key derivation and verification implementation
- `tests/conformance/epoch_key_derivation.rs` -- normative conformance tests
- `artifacts/10.14/epoch_key_vectors.json` -- published verification vectors (KAT)
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-3cs3/verification_evidence.json` -- machine-readable CI/release gate evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-3cs3/verification_summary.md` -- human-readable verification summary

## Dependencies

- Upstream: bd-3hdv (monotonic control epoch -- provides `ControlEpoch` type).
- Downstream: bd-2wsm (epoch transition barrier -- uses epoch-scoped keys for barrier authentication), bd-3epz (section gate), bd-5rh (section gate).","acceptance_criteria":"Authentication key derivation binds to epoch and domain; cross-epoch key reuse is impossible by construction; verification vectors are published.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"WildMountain","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:58.302630425Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T01:15:06.844350659Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T01:15:06.844314031Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-14","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3cs3","depends_on_id":"bd-3hdv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T16:24:18.167343306Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-3cvu","title":"[10.13 bug] Replace non-empty-token control-channel auth with transcript-bound capability verification","description":"Why this exists:
`crates/franken-node/src/connector/control_channel.rs` still authenticates control traffic with `!token.is_empty()`. That directly contradicts the closed claim that Section 10.13 delivered an authenticated control channel with anti-replay guarantees. Sequence monotonicity without real authenticity is not a secure protocol; it is a replay filter on top of ambient authority.

What this bead must do:
Replace the non-empty-token shortcut with a canonical transcript-bound capability verifier for control-channel frames. The implementation should not invent another ad hoc token format when the repo already contains stronger ingredients: audience/capability token machinery, epoch-scoped signing primitives, remote capability concepts, and canonical serializer infrastructure. The new control-channel auth path should bind identity, audience, session/channel ID, direction, sequence, payload hash, epoch/freshness window, and nonce into a domain-separated transcript that is actually verified. Use the graveyard ideas where they pay rent: attenuated capability tokens or macaroon-like caveats for least-authority control messages, persistent replay filters across restart boundaries, and deterministic counterexample traces for failed auth decisions.

Acceptance criteria:
1. `connector/control_channel.rs` no longer accepts arbitrary non-empty strings as authenticated control traffic; every accepted frame is backed by verified capability/session evidence.
2. Authentication binds at least: issuer/subject, audience, channel/session ID, direction, sequence number, payload commitment, freshness/epoch data, and nonce or equivalent replay salt.
3. Anti-replay state survives restart or is recoverably reconstructed from canonical state so an attacker cannot bypass replay protection with process restarts.
4. Capability attenuation is expressible: a control token/frame can be scoped to direction, action family, tenant/zone, and expiry, and verification enforces those caveats.
5. The implementation reuses canonical key/token/serialization components already present in-repo instead of spawning another incompatible auth mini-stack.
6. Adversarial fixtures cover guessed-token injection, payload swap under reused auth material, cross-channel replay, stale epoch reuse, restart-boundary replay, and caveat-bypass attempts.
7. Add a checker that fails if control-channel auth regresses to token-present / string-shape / opaque-bearer shortcuts.
8. Deliver unit tests, control-protocol replay fixtures, structured logs, and machine-readable evidence proving accepted frames are actually authenticated.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Control-channel authentication must verify transcript-bound capability evidence for every accepted frame and must persist or reconstruct replay state across restart boundaries; arbitrary non-empty bearer strings are never sufficient.
2. Authentication failures are user- and operator-explainable: denials emit stable error and reason codes, retryability hints, recovery hints, and enough transcript facts to debug without leaking secrets.
3. Unit tests cover transcript binding, audience enforcement, direction and action caveats, stale epoch rejection, restart-boundary replay rejection, nonce handling, and regressions against token-present shortcuts.
4. Integration tests exercise normal control traffic, attenuated capability scopes, restart recovery, cross-channel replay attempts, and invalid-token handling using deterministic vectors.
5. At least one deterministic operator E2E shell script under tests/e2e/ reproduces valid control traffic, guessed-token injection failure, replay failure after restart, and capability attenuation failure with detailed logs.
6. Structured logs expose stable CONTROL_AUTH_* event families with required fields: trace_id, channel_id, subject_id, audience, direction, sequence, decision, reason_code, retryable, and freshness_state.
7. Evidence artifacts under artifacts/replacement_gap/bd-3cvu/ include verification_evidence.json, verification_summary.md, protocol_vector_index.json, and any rch offload build IDs used for verification.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-09T00:19:58.103665746Z","created_by":"RoseMountain","updated_at":"2026-03-09T02:28:50.662959493Z","closed_at":"2026-03-09T02:28:50.662819933Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-optimization","extreme-optimization","gap-closure","replacement-critical","section-10-13"]}
